chunks: The Dambulla Cave Temple, also known as the Golden Temple of Dambulla, is Sri Lanka's largest and best-preserved cave temple complex. It is located in the Matale District, central Sri Lanka, cut into a massive granite outcrop 160 metres above the surrounding plains.

The Dambulla Cave Temple complex consists of five main caves, each converted into a Buddhist sanctuary. Together the caves contain 157 statues of the Buddha, three statues of Sri Lankan kings, and four statues of gods and goddesses. The murals inside cover approximately 2,100 square metres.

Cave 1, known as Devaraja Viharaya or the Cave of the Divine King, contains a 15-metre reclining Buddha carved entirely from natural rock. A wooden canopy structure surrounds the feet of the statue, depicting the scene of the Buddha's Parinirvana or final passing. Smaller images of seated Buddhas, Vishnu, and Saman are also housed here.

Cave 2, called Maharaja Viharaya or the Cave of the Great Kings, is the largest and most impressive of the five caves. It contains over 150 Buddha statues and its ceiling and walls are entirely covered in murals spanning approximately 2,100 square feet. Statues of King Valagamba and King Nissanka Malla are also present, acknowledging their patronage.

Cave 3, Maha Alut Viharaya or the New Great Cave, was built under the patronage of King Kirti Sri Rajasimha in the 18th century. It contains 50 gilt Buddha statues including a large reclining Buddha. Windows cut into the front wall admit natural light, illuminating the golden statues and the ceiling murals.

Cave 4, Pachima Viharaya or the Western Cave, is the smallest of the caves still used for worship. It houses approximately 10 seated Buddha statues and a central dagoba said to contain a jewel belonging to Queen Somawathie. The dagoba was reportedly opened by thieves in the early 20th century.

Cave 5, Devana Alut Viharaya or the Second New Cave, was added in the 18th century and is the newest of the five main caves. It contains a large reclining Buddha alongside statues of Hindu deities such as Vishnu and Kataragama, reflecting the traditional coexistence of Buddhism and Hinduism in Sri Lankan religious practice.

The Dambulla Cave Temple was first used as a place of refuge by King Valagamba (also written as Walagamba) in the 1st century BC, when he was driven from his throne in Anuradhapura by South Indian Chola invaders. Upon reclaiming his throne after 14 years, he converted the caves into royal rock temples in gratitude.

Dambulla was designated a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1991. The entire site including the rock and its surroundings was inscribed as the Golden Rock Temple of Dambulla. The site is listed under criterion i for outstanding universal value as a masterpiece of human creative genius.

The painted murals of Dambulla are considered among the finest examples of ancient Sri Lankan painting. The subjects include scenes from the Jataka stories (tales of the Buddha's previous lives), and episodes from the life of the historic Gautama Buddha. The style reflects influences from the Kandyan period of art.

King Nissanka Malla of the 12th century visited Dambulla and gilded many of the statues, referring to the site as Ran Giri or Golden Rock. The name Golden Temple of Dambulla is derived from this royal patronage. He also had inscriptions carved at the cave entrance recording his actions.

The Dambulla Rock rises to a height of approximately 160 metres and can be seen from a great distance across the flat Matale plains. A white-painted dagoba sits at the summit of the rock and can be seen from the town of Dambulla below.

The Dambulla Cave Temple is located approximately 72 kilometres north of Kandy and 148 kilometres north of Colombo, on the main Colombo-Trincomalee highway. Dambulla is also close to Sigiriya, which is located about 18 kilometres to the northeast.

Conservation and restoration work at Dambulla has been ongoing since the early 20th century. The murals are sensitive to humidity and light, and the Sri Lankan government along with UNESCO have worked to preserve them. Photography using flash was prohibited inside the caves to protect the ancient paintings.

The five caves of Dambulla range in size from small intimate chambers to large vaulted spaces. The ceilings of the caves follow the natural contours of the rock and are covered wall-to-wall with painted murals on layers of lime plaster applied to the rock surface over many centuries.
